architecture.summary: Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA) steers Llama 2 by computing steering vectors from residual stream activation differences between contrastive prompt pairs (positive vs. negative examples of a behavior). During inference, these vectors are added at all token positions after the user's prompt with a scalar multiplier, modulating the model's output distribution toward or away from the target behavior without retraining.

biblio.title: Steering Llama 2 via Contrastive Activation Addition

claims.statement: CAA significantly alters model behavior on multiple-choice behavioral evaluation datasets across all seven tested behaviors (AI Coordination, Corrigibility, Hallucination, Myopic Reward, Survival Instinct, Sycophancy, Refusal)

claims.dataset: open-ended generation tasks, rated by GPT-4
claims.statement: CAA generalizes from multiple-choice contrastive contexts to open-ended generation tasks
CAA can modify model behavior beyond what is achieved through system-prompting alone for most behaviors tested
For 3 out of 7 tested behaviors, CAA can additionally steer the behavior beyond the effects of supervised finetuning alone in both positive and negative directions

claims.metric: average probability assigned to correct answer
claims.statement: CAA intervention does not significantly affect MMLU performance
Optimal layers for steering with CAA correspond to layer 13 and adjacent layers in Llama 2 7B Chat, and layer 14-15 in Llama 2 13B Chat
Behavioral clustering in activation space emerges around one-third of the way through the layers, indicating that middle and later layers contain higher-level representations of behaviors
Generating a CAA steering vector requires less than five minutes on a single GPU, whereas finetuning Llama 2 7B Chat on 1000 examples requires 10 minutes on 2 NVIDIA L40 GPUs
